US: [306th](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population"), WI: [3rd](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Wisconsin "List of cities in Wisconsin")

is a city in [Brown County, Wisconsin](/wiki/Brown_County,_Wisconsin "Brown County, Wisconsin"), United States, and its [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat"). It is located along the [Green Bay inlet](/wiki/Green_Bay_(Lake_Michigan) "Green Bay (Lake Michigan)") at the mouth of the [Fox River](/wiki/Fox_River_(Green_Bay_tributary) "Fox River (Green Bay tributary)") on [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan"). With a population of 107,395 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), it is the [third-most populous city in Wisconsin](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Wisconsin "List of cities in Wisconsin") and [third-most populous city on Lake Michigan](/wiki/List_of_cities_on_the_Great_Lakes "List of cities on the Great Lakes"). The [Green Bay metropolitan area](/wiki/Green_Bay_metropolitan_area "Green Bay metropolitan area") has an estimated 335,000 residents.[[7]](#cite_note-USCensusQuickFacts-7)[[12]](#cite_note-PopEstCBSA-12)

**Green Bay** is a city in [Brown County, Wisconsin](/wiki/Brown_County,_Wisconsin "Brown County, Wisconsin"), United States, and its [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat"). It is located along the [Green Bay inlet](/wiki/Green_Bay_(Lake_Michigan) "Green Bay (Lake Michigan)") at the mouth of the [Fox River](/wiki/Fox_River_(Green_Bay_tributary) "Fox River (Green Bay tributary)") on [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan"). With a population of 107,395 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), it is the [third-most populous city in Wisconsin](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Wisconsin "List of cities in Wisconsin") and [third-most populous city on Lake Michigan](/wiki/List_of_cities_on_the_Great_Lakes "List of cities on the Great Lakes"). The [Green Bay metropolitan area](/wiki/Green_Bay_metropolitan_area "Green Bay metropolitan area") has an estimated 335,000 residents.[[7]](#cite_note-USCensusQuickFacts-7)[[12]](#cite_note-PopEstCBSA-12)

The Green Bay area was inhabited by various indigenous tribes, including the [Menominee](/wiki/Menominee "Menominee") and [Ho-Chunk](/wiki/Ho-Chunk "Ho-Chunk"). It was settled in 1634 by [Jean Nicolet](/wiki/Jean_Nicolet "Jean Nicolet") as a fur trading post in [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"). The first permanent European inhabitants arrived during [British control](/wiki/British_America "British America") in the mid-18th century. Its development was shaped by its location at the mouth of the Fox River, and the city emerged as a center for the [lumber](/wiki/History_of_the_lumber_industry_in_the_United_States "History of the lumber industry in the United States") and [paper industries](/wiki/Pulp_and_paper_industry_in_the_United_States "Pulp and paper industry in the United States") in the 19th and 20th centuries. Green Bay is also known for the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[Samuel de Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain"), the founder of [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"), commissioned [Jean Nicolet](/wiki/Jean_Nicolet "Jean Nicolet") to form a peaceful alliance with [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") in the western areas, whose unrest interfered with the French fur trade, and to search for a shorter trade route to China through Canada. Nicolet and others had learned from other First Nations of the [Ho-Chunk](/wiki/Ho-Chunk "Ho-Chunk") (Winnebago) people, who identified as "People of the Sea", and believed they must reside on or near an Ocean.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Champlain had also heard about natural resources in the area, including fertile soil, forests, and animals. Nicolet began his journey for this new land shortly before winter in 1634.[[14]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-14) In what later became a French fur-trading route, he sailed up the [Ottawa River](/wiki/Ottawa_River "Ottawa River"), through [Lake Nipissing](/wiki/Lake_Nipissing "Lake Nipissing") and down the [French River](/wiki/French_River_(Ontario) "French River (Ontario)") to [Lake Huron](/wiki/Lake_Huron "Lake Huron"), then through the straits of [Michilimackinac](/wiki/Michilimackinac "Michilimackinac") into Lake Michigan. He is believed to have landed at Red Banks, near the site of the modern-day city of Green Bay, Wisconsin.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### From the trading post *La Baie des Puants* to the town *La Baie verte* (1634–1761)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: From the trading post La Baie des Puants to the town La Baie verte (1634–1761)")]

Nicolet founded a small trading post here in 1634, originally named *La Baye* or *La Baie des Puants* (French for "the Bay of Stinking Waters").[[16]](#cite_note-Nicolet-16) Nicolet's settlement was one of the oldest European permanent settlements in America.[[17]](#cite_note-17) When Nicolet arrived in the Green Bay area, he encountered the [Menominee](/wiki/Menominee "Menominee"), who occupied this territory. He also met the [Ho-Chunk](/wiki/Ho-Chunk "Ho-Chunk") (also known as the Winnebago), a people who spoke a [Siouan language](/wiki/Siouan_language "Siouan language").

The Winnebago hunted and fished, and also cultivated corn, beans, squash, and tobacco. [Wild rice](/wiki/Wild_rice "Wild rice"), which they had incorporated as a dietary staple, grew in abundance along the riverbanks. The women regularly harvested and cooked this, along with a wide variety of nuts, berries, and edible roots which they gathered in the woods.[[18]](#cite_note-City_of_Green_Bay-18) The men typically hunted and fished for food, and the women processed game and other foods in cooking. They prepared and made clothing from the furs, as well as using other parts of animals to make tools, cord, etc. Women also had a role in the political process, as no action could be taken without agreement of half of the women. Nicolet stayed with this tribe for about a year, becoming an ally. He helped open up opportunities for trade and commerce with them before returning to Quebec.[[18]](#cite_note-City_of_Green_Bay-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Tourist%27s_Pocket_Map_Of_Michigan_%28Samuel_Augustus_Mitchell%2C_1835%29.jpg/3840px-Tourist%27s_Pocket_Map_Of_Michigan_%28Samuel_Augustus_Mitchell%2C_1835%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tourist%27s_Pocket_Map_Of_Michigan_(Samuel_Augustus_Mitchell,_1835).jpg)

Green Bay and Lake Winnebago on the 1835 *Tourist's Pocket Map of Michigan*, among the "[Mennomonie](/wiki/Mennomonie "Mennomonie")" villages of [Wisconsin Territory](/wiki/Wisconsin_Territory "Wisconsin Territory")

A few months after Nicolet returned to Quebec, Champlain died. His death halted other journeys to *La Baie Verte* (French for "The Green Bay"). Père [Claude Allouez](/wiki/Claude_Allouez "Claude Allouez") sent [Nicolas Perrot](/wiki/Nicolas_Perrot "Nicolas Perrot") to La Baie. After this, the French avoided the area for some decades, because of the intensity of First Nations and European conflicts in the east. In 1671, a [Jesuit](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") Mission was set up in the area. A fort was added in 1717 and gradually associated development took place. The town was incorporated in 1754. Great Britain took control of some French areas during the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"), known as the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War") in some areas of North America. They took control of this town in 1761. After the British defeated the French in 1763, France ceded its lands east of the Mississippi in North America.

The first permanent settlers were [Charles Michel de Langlade](/wiki/Charles_Michel_de_Langlade "Charles Michel de Langlade") and his family from Quebec, who moved to Green Bay in 1765. They are considered the first European settlers in the present-day state of Wisconsin. Langlade, called the "Founder and Father of Wisconsin", was a *[métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis "Métis")* or mixed-race, son of a French-Canadian father and an [Odawa](/wiki/Odawa "Odawa") woman. He grew up with his mother's family among the Odawa people and became a war chief. The Ottawa were allies of the French during the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War"), and Langlade is credited with planning the ambush of British General [Edward Braddock](/wiki/Edward_Braddock "Edward Braddock") and [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington"). His family was followed to Green Bay by the Grignons, Porliers and Lawes, who brought French-Canadian culture with them. Colorful "jack-knife Judge" Reaume dispensed British justice in the territory after Great Britain took it over following the war.[[18]](#cite_note-City_of_Green_Bay-18) These early ethnic French settlers set the tone for many who followed.

### British control (1761–1783)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: British control (1761–1783)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Tank_Cottage_Heritage_Hill_June_2014.jpg/250px-Tank_Cottage_Heritage_Hill_June_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tank_Cottage_Heritage_Hill_June_2014.jpg)

Built in 1776 by French-Canadian [voyageur](/wiki/Voyageurs "Voyageurs") Joseph Roi, the [Tank Cottage](/wiki/Tank_Cottage "Tank Cottage") is the [oldest standing building](/wiki/List_of_the_oldest_buildings_in_Wisconsin "List of the oldest buildings in Wisconsin") from the state's early years. Originally located on 8th Street along the [Fox River](/wiki/Fox_River_(Green_Bay_tributary) "Fox River (Green Bay tributary)"), the cottage was moved to [Heritage Hill State Historical Park](/wiki/Heritage_Hill_State_Historical_Park "Heritage Hill State Historical Park") in neighboring [Allouez](/wiki/Allouez,_Wisconsin "Allouez, Wisconsin") and is listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").[[19]](#cite_note-NRHP-19)

The British gradually took over Wisconsin during the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War"), taking control of Green Bay in 1761 and gaining control of all of Wisconsin in 1763. Like the French, the British were interested in little but the fur trade. The first permanent settlers, mostly [French Canadians](/wiki/French_Canadian "French Canadian"), some Anglo-New Englanders and a few African American freedmen, arrived in Wisconsin while it was under British control. [Charles Michel de Langlade](/wiki/Charles_Michel_de_Langlade "Charles Michel de Langlade") is generally recognized as the first settler, establishing a trading post at Green Bay in 1745, and moving there permanently in 1764.[[3]](#cite_note-settle-3)

Settlement began at Prairie du Chien around 1781. The French residents at the trading post in what is now Green Bay, referred to the town as "La Bey," however British fur traders referred to it as "Green Bay," because the water and the shore assumed green tints in early spring. The old French title was gradually dropped, and the British name of "Green Bay" stuck. The region coming under British rule had virtually no adverse effect on the French residents as the British needed the cooperation of the French fur traders and the French fur traders needed the goodwill of the British.

During the French occupation of the region licenses for fur trading had been issued scarcely and only to select groups of traders, whereas the British, in an effort to make as much money as possible from the region, issued licenses for fur trading freely, both to British and French residents. The fur trade in what is now Wisconsin reached its height under British rule, and the first self-sustaining farms in the state were established as well. From 1763 to 1780, Green Bay was a prosperous community which produced its own foodstuff, built graceful cottages and held dances and festivities.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In 1791, two free African Americans set up a fur trading post among the Menominee at present day [Marinette](/wiki/Marinette,_Wisconsin "Marinette, Wisconsin").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### After independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: After independence")]

The Green Bay area was still under British control until the 1783 treaty formally ended the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"). Following the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), which in part was over disputes related to the border with Canada, the United States built [Fort Howard](/wiki/Fort_Howard_(Wisconsin) "Fort Howard (Wisconsin)") on the [Fox River](/wiki/Fox_River_(Green_Bay_tributary) "Fox River (Green Bay tributary)") in 1816 to protect its northern border.[[18]](#cite_note-City_of_Green_Bay-18) Doty, Whitney, Arndt, Baird and Martin were among the many British-American settlers whose numbers pushed French culture into the background.[[18]](#cite_note-City_of_Green_Bay-18)

The [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") was completed in 1825, linking New England with the Great Lakes. This led to the advance of Green Bay as a trading center. The end of the [Black Hawk War](/wiki/Black_Hawk_War "Black Hawk War") in 1832 also gave impetus to settlement of the region. Most of the settlers were farmers from New England who began using the Erie Canal to pour into Wisconsin. As more and more New England settlers arrived, Green Bay developed into a trading center for this population.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/2009-0620-WI004-GB-Hazelwood.jpg/250px-2009-0620-WI004-GB-Hazelwood.jpg)](/wiki/File:2009-0620-WI004-GB-Hazelwood.jpg)

Built in 1837, the [Hazelwood Historic House Museum](/wiki/Hazelwood_(Green_Bay,_Wisconsin) "Hazelwood (Green Bay, Wisconsin)") is on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") and is now used as the Brown County Historical Society.[[22]](#cite_note-GBPG071609-22)

Wisconsin's first newspaper, *The [Green Bay Intelligencer](/wiki/Green_Bay_Intelligencer "Green Bay Intelligencer")*, was started in 1833 by [Albert Ellis](/wiki/Albert_Gallatin_Ellis "Albert Gallatin Ellis") and John V. Suydam. The borough of Green Bay, created in 1838, is the center of the present-day city. The borough combined the town of Astor (a company town of the [American Fur Company](/wiki/American_Fur_Company "American Fur Company")) with Navarino, platted by [Daniel Whitney](/wiki/Daniel_Whitney_(Wisconsin_entrepreneur) "Daniel Whitney (Wisconsin entrepreneur)").[[23]](#cite_note-23) Before Wisconsin became a state in 1848, its commerce was based on the [fur trade](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade"), which became dominated by [John Jacob Astor](/wiki/John_Jacob_Astor "John Jacob Astor")'s [American Fur Company](/wiki/American_Fur_Company "American Fur Company"). After statehood, there was a shift away from fur trading toward lumbering. "For a short time in 1860s and 1870s, iron smelting in charcoal kilns rivaled the timber industry while the port handled increasing amounts of fuel, feed, and lumber. Today's major local industry had its start in 1865 when the first paper mill was built."[[18]](#cite_note-City_of_Green_Bay-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Green_Bay_1867.jpg/250px-Green_Bay_1867.jpg)](/wiki/File:Green_Bay_1867.jpg)

1867 bird's eye illustration of Green Bay

By 1850 the town had a population of 1,923. The town was incorporated as the city of Green Bay on February 27, 1854.[[4]](#cite_note-f717-4) The [Green Bay Area Public School District](/wiki/Green_Bay_Area_Public_School_District "Green Bay Area Public School District") was founded in 1856.[[18]](#cite_note-City_of_Green_Bay-18) Throughout the 1850s, word spread of America's cheap land and good soil, bringing in an influx of [Belgian people](/wiki/Belgian_people "Belgian people"), German, Scandinavian, Irish and Dutch immigrants, each adding to the culture. The greatest concentration of newcomers came from Belgium. They cleared the land to farm and build their homes.[[18]](#cite_note-City_of_Green_Bay-18)

The railroad arrived in the 1860s. The three railroads that would reach Green Bay were the Chicago & North Western (C&NW), SOO Line, (SOO), and the Milwaukee Road (MILW). These railroads were highways which allowed people and products to travel all over the state, increasing business and trade opportunities. The area was able to grow and enrich itself with the use of the plentiful timber resources. This led to the paper industry becoming the major employer in Green Bay, and opened up the port for international trade.[[14]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-14)

Large numbers of Belgians immigrated to Green Bay in the thirty-year period between 1880 and 1910.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Significant numbers of English immigrants, many having lived first in Canada, also moved to Green Bay during this period, usually arriving as large families.[[25]](#cite_note-25) There was also a small Dutch community in Green Bay at this time.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Green Bay had a larger portion of first generation immigrants from France than any other city in Wisconsin at this time as well.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

The [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers"), an American Football team, were founded in 1919. They have been a member of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") since 1921, the only remaining "small town" team in any of the "[big four](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada")" major league sports leagues.

In 1934, President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") came to Green Bay to honor its tercentenary.[[16]](#cite_note-Nicolet-16) By 1950, the city had a population of 52,735. In 1964, the Town of [Preble](/wiki/Preble,_Wisconsin "Preble, Wisconsin") was consolidated with the city of Green Bay.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Geography")]

Green Bay is in the eastern part of Wisconsin at the mouth of the [Fox River](/wiki/Fox_River_(Green_Bay_tributary) "Fox River (Green Bay tributary)"). Today, [Interstate 43](/wiki/Interstate_43 "Interstate 43") meets [Interstate 41](/wiki/Interstate_41 "Interstate 41") (also [U.S. Route 41](/wiki/U.S._Route_41_in_Wisconsin "U.S. Route 41 in Wisconsin")) in Green Bay, approximately 90 miles (140 km) north of [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee").

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 55.76 square miles (144.4 km2), of which 45.48 square miles (117.8 km2) is land and 10.28 square miles (26.6 km2) is water.[[29]](#cite_note-gaz2020-29)

About 14% of the city of Green Bay is inside the [Oneida Nation of Wisconsin](/wiki/Oneida_Nation_of_Wisconsin "Oneida Nation of Wisconsin") reservation.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Green Bay has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Dfb*),[[31]](#cite_note-31) with some moderation due to the city's proximity to [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan"). Like other cities with this type of climate, there are four distinct [seasons](/wiki/Season "Season"), often with severe or extreme variation between them in terms of temperature and precipitation. Green Bay experiences warm, humid summers and cold, snowy winters. The variance in temperature and precipitation between months is severe and often extreme. [Tornadoes](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado") are rare in the Green Bay area, with the strongest being an [F3](/wiki/Fujita_scale "Fujita scale") tornado that hit the community of [Pittsfield](/wiki/Pittsfield,_Wisconsin "Pittsfield, Wisconsin") on June 26, 1969.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Monthly mean temperatures range from 16.6 °F (−8.6 °C) in January to 69.1 °F (20.6 °C) in July.[[33]](#cite_note-NOAA-33) In July, the warmest month, the average high temperature is 81.2 °F (27.3 °C).[[33]](#cite_note-NOAA-33) There are 6.1 days of 90 °F (32 °C)+ highs, 68 days where the high remains at or below freezing, and 19 days with sub-0 °F (−18 °C) lows annually. From December to February, even during thaws, the temperature rarely reaches 50 °F (10 °C). Extremes have ranged from −36 °F (−38 °C) on January 21, 1888, to 104 °F (40 °C) on July 13, 1936.

The wettest month in Green Bay is August, when 3.77 inches (96 mm) of precipitation falls, mostly in the form of rainfall from [thunderstorms](/wiki/Thunderstorm "Thunderstorm"). The driest month in Green Bay is February, when the majority of precipitation falls as low moisture-content snow due to cold, dry air. On average, 1.01 inches (26 mm) of precipitation falls in February.

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the [census of 2020](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"),[[37]](#cite_note-2020-census-5531000-38) the population was 107,395. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 2,361.4 inhabitants per square mile (911.7/km2). There were 45,789 housing units at an average density of 1,006.8 units per square mile (388.7 units/km2). ### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[45]](#cite_note-wwwcensusgov-48) of 2010, there were 104,057 people, 42,244 households, and 24,699 families residing in the city. The population density was 2,288.5 inhabitants per square mile (883.6/km2). There were 45,241 housing units at an average density of 995.0 per square mile (384.2/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 77.9% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 3.5% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 4.1% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 4.0% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.1% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Race_(U.S._Census) "Race (U.S. Census)"), 7.2% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(U.S._Census) "Race (U.S. Census)"), and 3.1% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") residents of any race were 13.4% of the population.

There were 42,244 households, of which 31.4% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 40.4% were married couples living together, 12.5% had a female householder with no husband present, 5.6% had a male householder with no wife present, and 41.5% were non-families. 32.4% of all households were made up of individuals, and 9.6% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.39 and the average family size was 3.06.

The median age in the city was 33.7 years. 24.7% of residents were under the age of 18; 11.7% were between the ages of 18 and 24; 27.7% were from 25 to 44; 24.5% were from 45 to 64; and 11.3% were 65 years of age or older. The gender makeup of the city was 49.4% male and 50.6% female.

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census") of 2000,[[10]](#cite_note-GR2-10) there were 102,313 people, 41,591 households, and 24,663 families residing in the city. The population density was 2,332.1 people per square mile (900.4 people/km2). There were 43,123 housing units at an average density of 982.9 units per square mile (379.5 units/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 85.9% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 1.4% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 3.3% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 3.8% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), <0.1% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._Census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. Census)"), 3.7% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(U.S._Census) "Race (U.S. Census)"), and 2.0% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") residents of any race were 7.1% of the population.

There were 41,591 households, of which 30.6% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 44.1% were married couples living together, 10.8% had a female householder with no husband present, and 40.7% were non-families. About 31.6% of all households were made up of individuals, and 9.9% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.40 and the average family size was 3.06.

In the city, the age distribution of the population shows 25.4% under the age of 18, 11.6% from 18 to 24, 31.7% from 25 to 44, 19.5% from 45 to 64, and 11.8% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 33 years. For every 100 females, there were 97.2 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 94.8 males.

The median income for a household in the city was $38,820, and the median income for a family was $48,678. Males had a median income of $33,246 versus $23,825 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the city was $19,269. About 7.4% of families and 10.5% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 12.7% of those under the age of 18 and 9.2% of those 65 and older.

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Saint_Francis_Xavier_Cathedral_Interior.jpg/250px-Saint_Francis_Xavier_Cathedral_Interior.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saint_Francis_Xavier_Cathedral_Interior.jpg)

[Saint Francis Xavier Cathedral](/wiki/Saint_Francis_Xavier_Cathedral_(Green_Bay,_Wisconsin) "Saint Francis Xavier Cathedral (Green Bay, Wisconsin)")

In 2000, the American Religion Data Archive reported Green Bay to be predominantly Catholic (71.5%), with [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") composing an additional 16.4%. The remaining 12% is almost entirely made-up of other Protestant denominations.[[46]](#cite_note-49)

The city is the seat of the [Roman Catholic Diocese of Green Bay](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Green_Bay "Roman Catholic Diocese of Green Bay"). The [Saint Francis Xavier Cathedral](/wiki/Saint_Francis_Xavier_Cathedral_(Green_Bay,_Wisconsin) "Saint Francis Xavier Cathedral (Green Bay, Wisconsin)") is the mother church of the Diocese which is in the province of the [Archdiocese of Milwaukee](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Milwaukee "Archdiocese of Milwaukee"). The [Saint Joseph Oratory](/wiki/Saint_Joseph_Oratory_(Green_Bay,_Wisconsin) "Saint Joseph Oratory (Green Bay, Wisconsin)") is in Green Bay. [St. Mary of the Angels Church and Monastery](/wiki/St._Mary_of_the_Angels_Church_and_Monastery "St. Mary of the Angels Church and Monastery") is also located in the city.

The [Wisconsin Evangelical Lutheran Synod](/wiki/Wisconsin_Evangelical_Lutheran_Synod "Wisconsin Evangelical Lutheran Synod") has four churches in Green Bay: St. Paul Lutheran Church,[[47]](#cite_note-50) First Evangelical Lutheran Church,[[48]](#cite_note-51) Beautiful Savior Lutheran Church,[[49]](#cite_note-52) and Messiah Lutheran Church.[[50]](#cite_note-53)[[51]](#cite_note-54) Christ the King Lutheran Church is a church of the [Evangelical Lutheran Synod](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Synod "Evangelical Lutheran Synod") in Green Bay.[[52]](#cite_note-55)

There are two Kingdom Halls of [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses") in the city, hosting 4 English congregations and a Spanish congregation.[[53]](#cite_note-56)

The Islamic Society of Wisconsin, Green Bay serves the Islamic community. The Green Bay Area Unitarian Universalist Fellowship is in the city. Congregation Cnesses Israel Temple, serving the area's Jewish population, is on the city's east side.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Georgia-Pacific_Broadway_facility_1.jpg/250px-Georgia-Pacific_Broadway_facility_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Georgia-Pacific_Broadway_facility_1.jpg)

[Georgia-Pacific](/wiki/Georgia-Pacific "Georgia-Pacific") Broadway paper mill

Green Bay was known as the "Toilet Paper Capital of the World" because of the prevalence of the paper industry in the city.[[57]](#cite_note-60) [Northern Paper Company](/wiki/Georgia-Pacific "Georgia-Pacific"), [Fort Howard Paper Company](/wiki/Fort_Howard_Paper_Company "Fort Howard Paper Company"), and [Hoberg Paper Company](/wiki/Charmin "Charmin") were among Green Bay's first paper companies. Northern Paper Mills, founded in Green Bay in 1901, became the largest producer of toilet paper in the world as Northern Tissue in 1920.[[58]](#cite_note-61) Northern Paper Company offered the first splinter-free [toilet paper](/wiki/Toilet_paper "Toilet paper") in the early 1930s.[[59]](#cite_note-62) The presence of the paper industry helped Green Bay avoid the worst effects of the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression").[[60]](#cite_note-63) Today, major paper producers include [Georgia-Pacific](/wiki/Georgia-Pacific "Georgia-Pacific")[[61]](#cite_note-64) and [Procter & Gamble](/wiki/Procter_%26_Gamble "Procter & Gamble"),[[62]](#cite_note-65) with niche companies such as Steen-Macek Paper Company.[[63]](#cite_note-66)

Among the earliest packing companies in Green Bay were [Acme Packing Company](/wiki/Acme_Packing_Company "Acme Packing Company") and [Indian Packing Company](/wiki/Indian_Packing_Company "Indian Packing Company"), the namesake of the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers").[[64]](#cite_note-67) Today, major meatpackers in the city include [JBS S.A.](/wiki/JBS_S.A. "JBS S.A.") (formerly Packerland Packing)[[65]](#cite_note-68) and American Foods Group.

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Meyer_Theatre_New_Marquee.jpg/250px-Meyer_Theatre_New_Marquee.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meyer_Theatre_New_Marquee.jpg)

[Meyer Theatre](/wiki/Meyer_Theatre "Meyer Theatre") marquee

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Packers_Heritage_Trail_Plaza.jpg/250px-Packers_Heritage_Trail_Plaza.jpg)](/wiki/File:Packers_Heritage_Trail_Plaza.jpg)

[Packers Heritage Trail](/wiki/Packers_Heritage_Trail "Packers Heritage Trail") Plaza

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Hotel_Northland.jpg/250px-Hotel_Northland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hotel_Northland.jpg)

[Hotel Northland](/wiki/Hotel_Northland "Hotel Northland")

The [Meyer Theatre](/wiki/Meyer_Theatre "Meyer Theatre"), [Tarlton Theatre](/wiki/Tarlton_Theatre "Tarlton Theatre"), and [Hotel Northland](/wiki/Hotel_Northland "Hotel Northland") are on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places"). The Northland was once the largest hotel in Wisconsin.[[69]](#cite_note-72)

The [Green Bay Film Festival](/wiki/Green_Bay_Film_Festival "Green Bay Film Festival") celebrates local and international filmmakers at The Tarlton Theatre, its home venue.[[70]](#cite_note-73)
Daddy D Productions performs at [Riverside Ballroom](/wiki/Riverside_Ballroom "Riverside Ballroom") and Let Me Be Frank Productions performs at the Meyer Theatre.[[71]](#cite_note-arts-74) The [Civic Symphony of Green Bay](/wiki/Civic_Symphony_of_Green_Bay "Civic Symphony of Green Bay") performs at the Meyer Theatre, its home venue. The Green Bay Jazz Orchestra performs at The Tarlton Theatre, its home venue. The former [Green Bay Symphony Orchestra](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay_Symphony_Orchestra&action=edit&redlink=1 "Green Bay Symphony Orchestra (page does not exist)") disbanded after their 2014–2015 season, after performing for over 100 years, citing financial difficulties.[[72]](#cite_note-75)

Other performance venues in Green Bay include [Lambeau Field](/wiki/Lambeau_Field "Lambeau Field"), [Resch Center](/wiki/Resch_Center "Resch Center"), and [Weidner Center](/wiki/Weidner_Center "Weidner Center").

The Art Garage[[73]](#cite_note-76) and the Automotive Gallery[[74]](#cite_note-77) are art galleries in the downtown area.[[71]](#cite_note-arts-74)

Museums in the city include the [Neville Public Museum](/wiki/Neville_Public_Museum_of_Brown_County "Neville Public Museum of Brown County") and the Hazelwood Historic House Museum.[[71]](#cite_note-arts-74) The [National Railroad Museum](/wiki/National_Railroad_Museum "National Railroad Museum") is located in the suburb of Ashwaubenon.[[75]](#cite_note-78)

Every summer, the downtown area plays host to ArtStreet, an art festival featuring studio displays, demonstrations, and live entertainment.[[76]](#cite_note-79) Dine on the Deck is an event that allows patrons to dine on the [CityDeck](/wiki/CityDeck "CityDeck") and features dishes from local restaurants.[[77]](#cite_note-80) Taste on Broadway has live entertainment and dishes served by local restaurants who compete for awards.[[78]](#cite_note-81) Artour brings all-original songwriters to downtown area venues.[[79]](#cite_note-82) IgNight hosts artisans, interactive art demonstrations, live entertainment, and life-size games.[[80]](#cite_note-83) [The Shipyard District](/wiki/The_Shipyard_District,_Inc. "The Shipyard District, Inc.") hosts the annual All Bands On Deck live music festival with bands at downtown bars and restaurants and free shuttles between venues.[[81]](#cite_note-84)[[82]](#cite_note-85)[[83]](#cite_note-86) [The Broadway District](/wiki/On_Broadway,_Inc. "On Broadway, Inc.") hosts a farmer's market every Wednesday from May to October.

### Shopping

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Shopping")]

Green Bay has one enclosed shopping mall, [East Town Mall](/wiki/East_Town_Mall "East Town Mall"), located within the city limits. The [Bay Park Square](/wiki/Bay_Park_Square "Bay Park Square") shopping mall is in the suburb of [Ashwaubenon](/wiki/Ashwaubenon,_Wisconsin "Ashwaubenon, Wisconsin"). The city was home to the first Shopko discount department store; it closed on April 22, 2019.[[84]](#cite_note-87)

### Public libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Public libraries")]

The Brown County Library (BCL) Central Branch is in downtown Green Bay and has served as the county public library since 1968. The Central Branch is the headquarters for the BCL system, which encompasses all public libraries in [Brown County](/wiki/Brown_County,_Wisconsin "Brown County, Wisconsin"), including eight branch libraries and a bookmobile that regularly visits locations throughout the county. In 1994, the Brown County Library was named Wisconsin Library of the Year.[[85]](#cite_note-88)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Green_Bay_July_2024_089_%28Green_Bay_City_Hall%29.jpg/250px-Green_Bay_July_2024_089_%28Green_Bay_City_Hall%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Green_Bay_July_2024_089_(Green_Bay_City_Hall).jpg)

City Hall

Green Bay is governed by a mayor and a city council. The mayor is elected in a citywide vote. Since 2019, the mayor of Green Bay is [Eric Genrich](/wiki/Eric_Genrich "Eric Genrich").[[88]](#cite_note-91) The city council consists of 12 members each elected from districts.

Green Bay is represented by [Tony Wied](/wiki/Tony_Wied "Tony Wied") (R) in the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), and by [Ron Johnson](/wiki/Ron_Johnson "Ron Johnson") (R) and [Tammy Baldwin](/wiki/Tammy_Baldwin "Tammy Baldwin") (D) in the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"). [Jamie Wall](/wiki/Jamie_Wall_(politician) "Jamie Wall (politician)") (D) and [Eric Wimberger](/wiki/Eric_Wimberger "Eric Wimberger") (R) represent Green Bay in the [Wisconsin State Senate](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Senate "Wisconsin State Senate"), and [Amaad Rivera-Wagner](/wiki/Amaad_Rivera-Wagner "Amaad Rivera-Wagner") (D), [Ryan Spaude](/wiki/Ryan_Spaude "Ryan Spaude") (D), and [Ben Franklin](/wiki/Ben_Franklin_(Wisconsin_politician) "Ben Franklin (Wisconsin politician)") (R) represent Green Bay in the [Wisconsin State Assembly](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Assembly "Wisconsin State Assembly").

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Cofrin_Library_sunset_2015.jpg/250px-Cofrin_Library_sunset_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cofrin_Library_sunset_2015.jpg)

Cofrin Library at [University of Wisconsin–Green Bay](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Green_Bay "University of Wisconsin–Green Bay")

Green Bay is served by the [Green Bay Area Public School District](/wiki/Green_Bay_Area_Public_School_District "Green Bay Area Public School District"), which covers almost all of its area.[[89]](#cite_note-2020CensusSDMap-92) It operates twenty-five elementary schools, two K–8 schools, four middle schools, four high schools, and one alternative school in the city and surrounding area. Two of the city's high schools, [East High School](/wiki/Green_Bay_East_High_School "Green Bay East High School") and [West High School](/wiki/Green_Bay_West_High_School "Green Bay West High School"), have [Wisconsin's longest consecutively-played high school football rivalry](/wiki/Green_Bay_East%E2%80%94Green_Bay_West_football_rivalry "Green Bay East—Green Bay West football rivalry"), played since 1905. Private schools in Green Bay include [Notre Dame de la Baie Academy](/wiki/Notre_Dame_de_la_Baie_Academy "Notre Dame de la Baie Academy"), [Northeastern Wisconsin Lutheran High School](/wiki/Northeastern_Wisconsin_Lutheran_High_School "Northeastern Wisconsin Lutheran High School"), and [Bay City Baptist School](/wiki/Bay_City_Baptist_School "Bay City Baptist School").

The [University of Wisconsin–Green Bay](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Green_Bay "University of Wisconsin–Green Bay") serves as a major public institution in the region, offering undergraduate and graduate degrees across a broad spectrum of disciplines. [Bellin College of Nursing](/wiki/Bellin_College_of_Nursing "Bellin College of Nursing") and [Northeast Wisconsin Technical College](/wiki/Northeast_Wisconsin_Technical_College "Northeast Wisconsin Technical College") are also based in the Green Bay area, while [College of Menominee Nation](/wiki/College_of_Menominee_Nation "College of Menominee Nation"), [Concordia University Wisconsin](/wiki/Concordia_University_Wisconsin "Concordia University Wisconsin"),[[90]](#cite_note-93) [Lakeland University](/wiki/Lakeland_University "Lakeland University"),[[91]](#cite_note-94) [Medical College of Wisconsin](/wiki/Medical_College_of_Wisconsin "Medical College of Wisconsin"),[[92]](#cite_note-95)[[93]](#cite_note-96) and [Rasmussen College](/wiki/Rasmussen_College "Rasmussen College") operate regional campuses or centers in the city.

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Old_Green_Bay_Railway_Station_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Old_Green_Bay_Railway_Station_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_Green_Bay_Railway_Station_-_panoramio.jpg)

[Green Bay station (Chicago and North Western Railway)](/wiki/Green_Bay_station_(Chicago_and_North_Western_Railway) "Green Bay station (Chicago and North Western Railway)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/78/Grb_air.jpg/250px-Grb_air.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grb_air.jpg)

[Green Bay–Austin Straubel International Airport](/wiki/Green_Bay%E2%80%93Austin_Straubel_International_Airport "Green Bay–Austin Straubel International Airport")

Green Bay is served by [Green Bay–Austin Straubel International Airport](/wiki/Green_Bay%E2%80%93Austin_Straubel_International_Airport "Green Bay–Austin Straubel International Airport"), located in [Ashwaubenon](/wiki/Ashwaubenon,_Wisconsin "Ashwaubenon, Wisconsin") just west of the city. [Green Bay Metro](/wiki/Green_Bay_Metro "Green Bay Metro") provides mass transit bus service throughout Green Bay and the surrounding suburbs. [Amtrak Thruway](/wiki/Amtrak_Thruway "Amtrak Thruway"), [Indian Trails](/wiki/Indian_Trails "Indian Trails"), Lamers Bus Lines, and [Wisconsin Coach Lines](/wiki/Wisconsin_Coach_Lines "Wisconsin Coach Lines") provide intercity transportation from the central Green Bay Metro station downtown. The Port of Green Bay handled 1.99 million tons of cargo in 2015, with the primary shipments into and out of the port including coal, limestone, salt, and cement.[[95]](#cite_note-port-98)

In April 2020, Green Bay Metro partnered with [Via Transportation](/wiki/Via_Transportation "Via Transportation") to launch GBM Paratransit, a service for riders with disabilities not served by traditional fixed-route transit; eligible users must apply online and can schedule rides through the GBM Paratransit app or by phone.[[96]](#cite_note-99) Following its success, Green Bay Metro and Via introduced GBM On Demand in August 2020, the first micro-transit service in Wisconsin.[[97]](#cite_note-Schmidt-100)

From 1896 to 1993 the city was the headquarters of the [Green Bay and Western Railroad](/wiki/Green_Bay_and_Western_Railroad "Green Bay and Western Railroad"). In 1993, the line was purchased by the [Wisconsin Central](/wiki/Wisconsin_Central_Ltd. "Wisconsin Central Ltd."). In 2001, the WC was merged into the [Canadian National Railway](/wiki/Canadian_National_Railway "Canadian National Railway"). The [Chicago and North Western Railway](/wiki/Chicago_and_North_Western_Transportation_Company "Chicago and North Western Transportation Company") also served Green Bay and its [depot](/wiki/Chicago_and_North_Western_Railway_Passenger_Depot_(Green_Bay) "Chicago and North Western Railway Passenger Depot (Green Bay)") still stands. Green Bay was last served with a regular passenger train, the CNW's *[Peninsula 400](/wiki/Peninsula_400 "Peninsula 400"),* in 1971. The CNW sold its trackage from Green Bay south to Sheboygan in 1987 to the [Fox River Valley Railroad](/wiki/Fox_River_Valley_Railroad "Fox River Valley Railroad"), which became part of the WC in 1993. Green Bay also saw passenger service from the [Milwaukee Road](/wiki/Chicago,_Milwaukee,_St._Paul_and_Pacific_Railroad "Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad")'s [Chippewa-Hiawatha](/wiki/Chippewa-Hiawatha "Chippewa-Hiawatha"), which ran from Chicago via the [Milwaukee Road depot](/wiki/Green_Bay_station_(Milwaukee_Road) "Green Bay station (Milwaukee Road)") into the [upper peninsula of Michigan](/wiki/Upper_peninsula_of_Michigan "Upper peninsula of Michigan"). Green Bay is also served by the [Escanaba and Lake Superior Railroad](/wiki/Escanaba_and_Lake_Superior_Railroad "Escanaba and Lake Superior Railroad").

Currently, there is no passenger rail service to Green Bay, but there is a proposal to bring [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") train service by extending the route of the *[Hiawatha](/wiki/Hiawatha_(Amtrak_train) "Hiawatha (Amtrak train)")*, which currently goes from Chicago to Milwaukee, north to Green Bay.[[98]](#cite_note-101)

* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/WIS_54.svg/40px-WIS_54.svg.png)](/wiki/File:WIS_54.svg) [WIS 54](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_54 "Wisconsin Highway 54") travels east to [Algoma](/wiki/Algoma,_Wisconsin "Algoma, Wisconsin"), and west towards [Seymour](/wiki/Seymour_(city),_Outagamie_County,_Wisconsin "Seymour (city), Outagamie County, Wisconsin").
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/WIS_57.svg/40px-WIS_57.svg.png)](/wiki/File:WIS_57.svg) [WIS 57](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_57 "Wisconsin Highway 57") travels north towards [Sturgeon Bay](/wiki/Sturgeon_Bay,_Wisconsin "Sturgeon Bay, Wisconsin"), and south towards Milwaukee.
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/WIS_172.svg/40px-WIS_172.svg.png)](/wiki/File:WIS_172.svg) [WIS 172](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_172 "Wisconsin Highway 172") begins at I-43 in [Bellevue](/wiki/Bellevue,_Wisconsin "Bellevue, Wisconsin") and travels west to [Hobart](/wiki/Hobart,_Wisconsin "Hobart, Wisconsin").

### Utilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Utilities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/ISS-38_Green_Bay%2C_Wisconsin.jpg/250px-ISS-38_Green_Bay%2C_Wisconsin.jpg)](/wiki/File:ISS-38_Green_Bay,_Wisconsin.jpg)

Open water appears near the mouth of the Fox due to the warmth of the water coming from [J. P. Pulliam Generating Station](/wiki/J._P._Pulliam_Generating_Station "J. P. Pulliam Generating Station"), 2014

Green Bay is served by [Wisconsin Public Service Corporation](/wiki/Wisconsin_Public_Service_Corporation "Wisconsin Public Service Corporation"). The [J. P. Pulliam Generating Station](/wiki/J._P._Pulliam_Generating_Station "J. P. Pulliam Generating Station") has been demolished[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*].[[99]](#cite_note-102) Water service is provided to the city by the Green Bay Water Utility.[[100]](#cite_note-103) Sewer service is provided by the Green Bay Metropolitan Sewerage District, also known as NEW Water.[[101]](#cite_note-104)

### Health care

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Green_Bay,_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Health care")]

Green Bay is the headquarters of [Bellin Health](/wiki/Bellin_Health "Bellin Health") and Prevea Health, regional [health care](/wiki/Health_care "Health care") providers.[[102]](#cite_note-105)

Green Bay is home to four hospitals: Aurora Baycare Medical Center, [Bellin Hospital](/wiki/Bellin_Health "Bellin Health"), [St. Mary's Hospital Medical Center](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Hospital_Medical_Center "St. Mary's Hospital Medical Center"), and [St. Vincent Hospital](/wiki/St._Vincent_Hospital "St. Vincent Hospital"). Green Bay is also home to the Milo C. Huempfner VA Outpatient Clinic, and [Bellin Psychiatric Center](/wiki/Bellin_Health "Bellin Health") and Willow Creek Behavioral Health, the city's two [psychiatric hospitals](/wiki/Psychiatric_hospital "Psychiatric hospital").[[103]](#cite_note-106)
